new_terms_proposed.term_label: Victim of Domestic Violence or Sexual Assault
new_terms_proposed.notes: Covers employees who are victims (or whose family members are victims) of domestic or sexual violence taking leave under the SAFE Act. No existing child slug covers this.

meta.json_run_notes: Generated 5 statute records from the provided source texts (CEPA, NJFCA, Wage Theft Act, SAFE Act, Workers' Compensation Act). The run scope requested 7 records, but only 5 distinct statutes could be confidently identified in the reference materials. Excluded all case law citations because verifiable URLs to approved sources could not be confirmed from the raw text alone.

legal_basis.adverse_action_scope: Broadly defined as discharge, suspension, demotion, or any other adverse employment action taken against an employee in the terms and conditions of employment.

statute_of_limitations.exhaustion_details: Written notice to a supervisor is generally required for external disclosures to a public body, allowing the employer a reasonable opportunity to correct the activity.

records._review_notes: CEPA is New Jersey's primary whistleblower statute. Incorporates strict 1-year SOL and requires written notice to a supervisor for external disclosures unless an emergency exception applies.

legal_basis.adverse_action_scope: Includes discharge, demotion, suspension, threats, harassment, or discrimination in any other manner in the terms and conditions of employment.

reward.reward_details: Relator receives between 15-25% of recovery if the Attorney General intervenes, and 25-30% if they proceed independently.

records._review_notes: Statute offers double back pay and a generous 3-year SOL. Rewards for qui tam interventions are codified.

legal_basis.adverse_action_scope: Discharge, discrimination concerning compensation or conditions of employment, or retaliatory discharge based on complaining about unpaid wages or wage calculation.

statute_of_limitations.limit_details: The 6-year period applies to general wage claims. Retaliation presumption specifically applies to actions within 90 days of an employee filing a complaint.

burden_of_proof.employer_defense_details: To overcome the 90-day presumption, an employer must provide clear and convincing evidence that the action was taken for other permissible reasons.

records._review_notes: Significantly updated by the 2019 amendments. Includes a 90-day rebuttable presumption of retaliation requiring an elevated clear and convincing standard for the employer defense.

records.official_name: New Jersey Security and Financial Empowerment Act

legal_basis.protected_class_details: Employees who are victims of domestic violence or sexual assault, or whose family members are victims, taking protected leave.
legal_basis.adverse_action_scope: Discharge, harassment, threats, or discrimination in compensation or terms/conditions of employment.

records._review_notes: Provides job security and 20 days of unpaid leave for victims of domestic/sexual violence. Contains explicit anti-retaliation provisions for invoking leave rights.

legal_basis.adverse_action_scope: Discharge or any manner of discrimination as to employment for claiming or attempting to claim workers' compensation benefits.

statute_of_limitations.limit_details: Derived from the standard two-year statute of limitations applying generally to workers' compensation cases.

records._review_notes: Prohibits retaliation for filing workers comp claims. Allows for both administrative/criminal penalties under statute and a common law cause of action for civil damages based on case law.

error_details: record_count is less than records_requested (5 found in source, 7 requested).
Citations omitted because verifiable URLs could not be confirmed from approved sources.
NJ-34:11-4.10: SOL value derived from general wage claim provisions — limit_ambiguous: true.
NJ-34:15-39.1: SOL value derived from standard workers compensation claim deadlines — limit_ambiguous: true.
